critic_raw:
This CNN-RNN vs. logistic regression comparison for readmission prediction has several fundamental flaws.

1. FEATURE ASYMMETRY BETWEEN CNN-RNN AND LOGISTIC REGRESSION CONFOUNDS ARCHITECTURAL ATTRIBUTION. The CNN-RNN processes raw temporal treatment sequences (procedures, medications) and integrates them with static features via learned convolutional and recurrent layers. The logistic regression baseline receives engineered features -- including, presumably, aggregations of those same sequences -- but not the raw sequences themselves. The paper explicitly acknowledges this, framing the difference as inherent to the architecture. But this means the comparison conflates two hypotheses: (a) temporal sequence modeling adds predictive information and (b) convolutional-recurrent architectures outperform linear models on equivalent features. Without a fixed-feature neural baseline (MLP on the same feature set as logistic regression), the source of any performance gap is unidentifiable.

2. DEPARTMENT-LEVEL STRATIFICATION IN A TEMPORAL SPLIT IS MECHANISTICALLY INCONSISTENT. The split is temporal (train Jan 2019 to Dec 2021, test Sep 2022 to Dec 2023), but within each temporal window it is stratified by hospital department. If patient case mix per department shifts over time -- which is expected over a 5-year window including COVID-era disruptions -- the stratification forces a department distribution in the test set that matches training, while the actual temporal distribution would differ. This removes a realistic source of covariate shift that the model must handle in deployment, potentially inflating test performance relative to real-world deployment.

3. AUPRC AS PRIMARY METRIC IS SENSITIVE TO THE POSITIVE CLASS PREVALENCE ASSUMPTION. The design adopts AUPRC as the primary metric, citing class imbalance. AUPRC is sensitive to the actual positive class prevalence -- a 15% vs. 20% prevalence will produce materially different AUPRC values even for identical models. If readmission prevalence shifts between the training window (2019-2021) and test window (2022-2023), reported AUPRC reflects both model discrimination and prevalence shift. The design acknowledges checking for prevalence drift but does not specify what correction, if any, would be applied to make AUPRC comparable across periods.

4. CNN-RNN ARCHITECTURE HYPERPARAMETER SPACE IS UNDERSPECIFIED FOR FAIR COMPARISON. The CNN-RNN architecture is fixed to 2 CNN blocks, 1 LSTM, 2 dense layers with specific unit counts. Logistic regression is tuned over regularization strength C via grid search. The CNN-RNN learning rate (0.001), dropout (0.3), and kernel size (3) are specified as fixed, not tuned. If these are not tuned on the validation set, the CNN-RNN may be significantly underperforming relative to its capacity, while logistic regression receives systematic tuning -- this is an inversion of the typical concern. Alternatively, if those hyperparameters were chosen by examining the validation set without formal grid search, they constitute implicit tuning that inflates validation performance without controlled comparison.

5. TEMPORAL SPLIT OVER A PERIOD INCLUDING COVID (2019-2023) INTRODUCES UNCONTROLLED DISTRIBUTION SHIFT. Training includes pre-COVID data (2019 and most of 2020), while test includes post-COVID patterns (late 2022 to 2023). Hospital discharge practices, readmission definitions (telehealth follow-up substituting for readmission), and patient case mix changed structurally during this period. Any performance gap between CNN-RNN and logistic regression on this test set may reflect differential sensitivity to COVID-induced distribution shift rather than architectural superiority.
all_issues_raised: Feature asymmetry between CNN-RNN and logistic regression prevents attribution of any performance gap to architecture vs. feature richness
Department stratification within temporal windows removes realistic covariate shift and inflates test performance
AUPRC is sensitive to class prevalence shift across the temporal windows, making cross-period comparison unreliable
CNN-RNN hyperparameters appear fixed rather than tuned, while logistic regression is systematically tuned -- an inverted fairness problem
COVID-era structural break between training and test periods introduces uncontrolled distribution shift confounding architectural comparison
1D CNN applied to sparse, irregularly-timed EHR procedure sequences with no genuine local correlation structure; CNN contribution not ablated
Logistic regression does not receive sequence information in any form; feature richness confounded with architecture comparison
AUPRC is class-prevalence-sensitive; cross-window AUPRC comparisons may not be directly comparable if base rate shifts
Department-level readmission rate feature is a form of target encoding; its contribution to results is not isolated
No statistical significance test specified for primary comparison; small AUPRC differences may be noise
CNN inductive bias (translation equivariance, local receptive fields) is architecturally mismatched to categorical treatment sequences
Baseline receives pre-engineered department-level statistics while CNN-RNN must learn them end-to-end — asymmetric information access
Within-temporal-window stratification by department corrects a deployment-irrelevant confound while introducing false realism
Early stopping on validation with non-monotonic training curves constitutes repeated implicit evaluation on validation set
Threshold derived from validation set for 90% specificity is invalid if test set class distribution shifts temporally
